what:
A language package for working with MongoDb. The adaptor lets OpenFn workflows read from and write to MongoDB Atlas / Driver.

It exposes 3 operations that you can call from a workflow step, listed in the Operations tab. 1 ready-to-paste snippets are grouped as create (1). Vendor homepage: https://www.mongodb.com.
connect:
MongoDB supports HTTP Basic auth, API keys, or bearer tokens for API access.

The credential schema asks for clusterHostname (required), username (required), password (required), protocol, and options. Point the credential at the base URL that matches your environment: mongodb+srv://<cluster>.mongodb.net, https://data.mongodb-api.com/app/<app-id>/endpoint/data/v1. Auth reference: https://www.mongodb.com/docs/atlas/app-services/data-api/authenticate/.
when:
Reach for this adaptor when your workflow needs to read or write data in MongoDB Atlas / Driver.

4 triggers are catalogued (0 webhook, 4 polling), including New Collection, New Database, New Document, New Field. See the Triggers tab for the full list. MongoDB can push events to OpenFn via webhooks (docs: https://www.mongodb.com/docs/atlas/app-services/triggers/), so reactive workflows are a good fit.
wire:
A typical wiring pattern is trigger → fetch → transform → write.

Kick the workflow off from a trigger on the Triggers tab (webhook-driven where supported, polling otherwise). Then chain operations from this adaptor's Operations tab to fetch or write data in MongoDB. Pass values between steps through state.data — every operation reads from and writes to job state.

To move data between systems, follow this step with another adaptor (e.g. write the fetched records into a downstream FHIR, DHIS2, or database adaptor).
scale:
When running MongoDB jobs at volume:

- Prefer MongoDB webhooks over polling — they cut latency and rate-limit pressure.
- Batch requests where the API supports it and avoid fan-out loops over large collections.
- Make jobs idempotent by keying on stable external IDs so a replay doesn't create duplicates.
- Surface 4xx/5xx responses with the vendor request ID in your logs, and treat 429s as retries with backoff.

Adapter note: Primary access is via the native driver over the Mongo wire protocol; Atlas also exposes a REST Data API.
best:
- Store MongoDB credentials in OpenFn's credential store; never inline keys or tokens in job code.
- Pin @openfn/language-mongodb to v2.1.29 in package.json so an upstream change doesn't break a production workflow silently.
- Keep each step small — one logical API call per operation makes failures easier to diagnose and retry.
- Add defensive checks before transforming inbound payloads — vendor responses change between versions.
- Log enough context (resource id, run id, vendor request id) so an incident can be traced end-to-end.
